content:
First, note that any \(A_i\) must be an integer, and 
\[
\sum_{i=1}^7 a_i \;=\;19,\qquad 
\sum_{i=1}^7 A_i\;=\;19.
\]
Since each \(a_i\in(2.5,3)\), any optimal \(A_i\) will be either 2 or 3.  Writing
\[
x=\#\{i:A_i=3\},\quad 7-x=\#\{i:A_i=2\},
\]
we need
\[
3x+2(7-x)=19\;\Longrightarrow\;14+x=19\;\Longrightarrow\;x=5,
\]
so exactly five of the \(A_i\) are 3 and two are 2.

To minimize the worst error \(M=\max_i|A_i-a_i|\), we should choose the two smallest \(a_i\) to get rounded down to 2.  The two smallest are
\[
a_1=2.56,\quad a_2=2.61,
\]
so set \(A_1=A_2=2\) and \(A_3,\dots,A_7=3\).  The errors are
\[
|2-2.56|=0.56,\quad |2-2.61|=0.61,
\]
and for the others
\[
|3-2.65|=0.35,\;|3-2.71|=0.29,\;|3-2.79|=0.21,\;|3-2.82|=0.18,\;|3-2.86|=0.14.
\]
Thus 
\[
M=\max\{0.56,0.61,0.35,0.29,0.21,0.18,0.14\}=0.61,
\]
and
\[
100M=61.
\]
Hence the answer is \(\boxed{61}.\)
